transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Naomi. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about how I feel when I’m not at work, especially when I run into coworkers. It’s just... I feel this pressure, like I have to prove that I’m really sick or something. It makes me anxious, you know? I worry about what they think of me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re experiencing a lot of anxiety when you encounter your coworkers outside of work, especially regarding how they perceive your health. That pressure to prove yourself must be really challenging. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you feel that way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... when I see them, I can't help but think they might judge me. Like, they’ll think I’m faking it or that I’m not really sick. I guess I just feel guilty for not being at work, even if I know I need to take care of myself. It’s like this constant worry that I’m letting everyone down, you know? I don’t want to be seen as unreliable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy burden of guilt and worry about how your coworkers perceive you, especially regarding your health and absence from work. You mentioned feeling like you might be letting everyone down and not wanting to be seen as unreliable. That must create a lot of internal conflict for you. Can you share more about where those feelings of guilt and worry come from? Have you experienced similar feelings in the past?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I’ve always been someone who wants to do well and be dependable. I mean, I’ve had moments in the past where I felt like I wasn’t living up to expectations, and it really stuck with me. It’s like I’ve built this idea that if I’m not always present or performing, then I’m failing somehow. I don’t know... maybe it’s just this fear of disappointing others that keeps coming back. It’s hard to shake off, especially when I’m already feeling unwell.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like your desire to be dependable and meet expectations has been a significant part of your identity, and it’s understandable that those past experiences of feeling like you weren’t living up to those expectations would stick with you. The fear of disappointing others sounds like it’s deeply rooted and can be especially tough to manage when you’re already feeling unwell. How do you think this fear influences your day-to-day life, especially when you’re not at work?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely makes everything feel more complicated. Even when I’m at home trying to rest, I can’t fully relax because I’m constantly worrying about what others might think. It’s like I’m always on edge, second-guessing myself. I find myself thinking I should be doing more, being more proactive about my situation, even though I know I need to take it easy. It’s exhausting, really. I feel like I’m stuck in this cycle of guilt and anxiety, and it just makes everything harder.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds incredibly exhausting, Naomi. It’s like you’re caught in a cycle where your need to rest and take care of yourself conflicts with the pressure you feel to be more proactive and meet others' expectations. It must be really frustrating to feel that way, especially when you know you need to prioritize your health. How do you usually cope with those feelings of guilt and anxiety when they arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I try to seek confirmation from others, like asking friends or family if they think I should take more time off. But even then, I find myself doubting their support, like maybe they’re just saying what I want to hear. I also spend a lot of time looking up HR policies online, hoping to find some clear guidance, but it just adds to my stress. Sometimes, I even feel the urge to argue against supportive comments, like I’m judging myself for needing help. It’s like I can’t accept that it’s okay to take care of myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a difficult place where seeking reassurance from others doesn’t bring you the comfort you need, and instead, it leads to more self-doubt. The urge to argue against supportive comments and the struggle to accept that it’s okay to take care of yourself must be really challenging. It’s almost as if you’re in a battle with yourself over what you need versus what you feel you should be doing. What do you think makes it so hard for you to accept that it’s okay to prioritize your own well-being?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s this deep-rooted belief that I should always be strong and capable. I’ve always felt like I need to prove myself, and taking time for myself feels like I’m not living up to that standard. There’s this voice in my head that tells me if I enjoy my time away from work, then I’m being lazy or taking advantage of the situation. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against what I think others expect of me, and it’s hard to let go of that. I guess I just worry that if I prioritize my well-being, it’ll somehow make me look weak or unworthy.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a strong internal narrative that equates strength and capability with constant productivity and self-sacrifice. That belief seems to create a lot of pressure for you, making it difficult to enjoy time for yourself without feeling guilty or lazy. It’s understandable that you would worry about how prioritizing your well-being might be perceived by others, especially when you’ve held onto those expectations for so long. How do you feel about that voice in your head? Does it ever change, or is it pretty consistent in how it speaks to you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s pretty consistent, unfortunately. That voice is always there, reminding me of what I should be doing or how I should be feeling. It feels like a constant companion, and it’s hard to silence it. Sometimes, I try to challenge it, but it usually just ends up making me feel worse. I guess I wish it would be more supportive, but it’s like it’s stuck on this loop of criticism. It makes it really hard to break free from those thoughts and just allow myself to feel okay about needing time off.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to have that critical voice as a constant presence in your life, especially when it makes it challenging to feel okay about your needs. It’s understandable that trying to challenge it often leads to feeling worse rather than better. That loop of criticism can be so draining, and it seems to keep you from finding the support and compassion you deserve, both from yourself and from others. If you could imagine a different kind of voice—one that is more supportive—what do you think it might say to you in moments like this?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think a more supportive voice would remind me that it’s okay to take a break and that my health is important. It might say something like, “You’re doing the best you can, and it’s okay to rest. Taking care of yourself doesn’t mean you’re weak; it means you’re human.” I guess it would encourage me to be kinder to myself and to recognize that needing time off doesn’t define my worth. It would help me see that everyone has their struggles and that it’s okay to prioritize my well-being without feeling guilty. That kind of message would feel really comforting, but it’s hard to access that voice when I’m so caught up in the critical one.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That supportive voice you described sounds really nurturing and compassionate. It emphasizes your humanity and the importance of self-care, which is such a vital message to hear, especially during tough times. It’s understandable that accessing that kinder perspective can be difficult when the critical voice is so loud. It seems like there’s a part of you that recognizes the need for self-compassion, even if it feels out of reach right now. What do you think might help you begin to cultivate that supportive voice more often, even in small ways?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think starting with small steps could help, like reminding myself that it’s okay to take breaks and not feel guilty about it. Maybe I could try writing down some affirmations or positive reminders to counter that critical voice. I could also practice talking to myself like I would to a friend who’s struggling—being more understanding and patient. It might take time, but I think if I consciously try to be kinder to myself, it could help shift that internal dialogue a bit. Just finding ways to acknowledge my feelings without judgment could be a start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really meaningful steps, Naomi. Writing down affirmations and practicing self-talk as if you were comforting a friend could be powerful tools for nurturing that supportive voice. It’s great that you’re recognizing the importance of acknowledging your feelings without judgment. It sounds like you’re beginning to create a path toward greater self-compassion, even if it takes time. How do you feel about starting with these small steps? What might be the first one you’d like to try?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, honestly. Starting with writing down some affirmations seems like a manageable first step. I think I could set aside a little time to reflect on what I really need to hear and write those down. Just having those words to look at might help me when I start feeling overwhelmed or guilty. It feels like a gentle way to remind myself that it’s okay to take care of myself. I’m a bit nervous about it, but I think it could be a good start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful about this, and setting aside time to reflect and write down affirmations sounds like a gentle and nurturing approach. It’s completely normal to feel a bit nervous, especially when trying something new, but it also seems like you’re taking an important step toward being kinder to yourself. Remember, it’s all about progress, not perfection. How do you envision incorporating this practice into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could try to set aside a few minutes each morning, maybe right after I wake up or with my morning coffee. That way, I can start the day with a positive mindset. I might even keep the affirmations somewhere visible, like on my mirror or in my planner, so I can see them throughout the day. I think having that reminder could help me stay grounded, especially when those critical thoughts start creeping in. It feels like a small but meaningful way to shift my focus and give myself a little more grace.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a lovely plan, Naomi. Starting your day with those affirmations and keeping them visible can create a positive foundation for your mindset. It’s great that you’re thinking about ways to stay grounded throughout the day, especially when those critical thoughts arise. Giving yourself that grace is so important. How do you feel about sharing your progress or any challenges you encounter as you start this practice? Would that be something you’d find helpful?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think sharing my progress could be really helpful. It might keep me accountable and also give me a chance to reflect on how I’m feeling as I try this out. I know there might be challenges along the way, but having someone to talk to about it could make it feel less daunting. I guess it would be nice to have support as I work on being kinder to myself. I’m open to checking in about it and seeing how it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great approach, Naomi. Having someone to share your progress and challenges with can definitely provide support and accountability, making the journey feel less daunting. It’s wonderful that you’re open to checking in and reflecting on how this practice unfolds for you. Remember, it’s all about taking it one step at a time. Is there anything else on your mind today, or any other areas you’d like to explore further?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve shared a lot about what’s been weighing on me, and I appreciate the space to talk about it. I guess I just want to make sure I’m being kind to myself as I navigate this whole situation with work and my health. It feels like there’s a lot to unpack, but I’m hopeful that these small steps can lead to some positive changes. I’m just grateful for the opportunity to explore this today. Thank you for listening.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad you felt comfortable sharing what’s been weighing on you, Naomi. It’s clear that you’re taking thoughtful steps toward being kinder to yourself, and that hopefulness is such an important part of this journey. Remember, it’s okay to take your time with this process, and I’m here to support you as you navigate it. Thank you for being open and sharing your thoughts today. If you ever want to explore more or check in about your progress, I’m here for you. Take care of yourself, and I wish you all the best as you start this new practice.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much. I really appreciate your support and understanding. It feels good to have a space to talk about these things, and I’m looking forward to trying out the affirmations. I’ll definitely keep in mind that I can check in about my progress. Take care, and I hope to talk again soon.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Naomi. I’m glad you found this space helpful, and I’m excited for you as you start working with the affirmations. Remember, it’s all about progress, and I’m here whenever you want to check in or share your experiences. Take care of yourself, and I look forward to our next conversation. Wishing you all the best!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That means a lot to me. I’ll definitely keep that in mind, and I look forward to our next conversation as well. Take care, and I hope to talk soon!
